content: A Fixed Deposit (FD) is a savings product offered by banks and NBFCs where you deposit a lump sum for a fixed period at a guaranteed interest rate. Unlike savings accounts, the interest rate does not change during the tenure. Your money is locked in, but you earn more interest. FDs are one of the safest investments in India.

FD interest can be simple or compound. Simple interest: Interest = P × R × T / 100. Compound interest (quarterly): Maturity = P × (1 + R/(4×100))^(4×T). Most Indian banks compound quarterly. For example, Rs 1,00,000 at 7% for 1 year compounded quarterly gives Rs 1,07,186. The more frequently interest compounds, the more you earn.

FD tenures in India range from 7 days to 10 years. Short-term FDs (7 days to 1 year) offer lower rates. Medium-term (1-3 years) offer moderate rates. Long-term (3-10 years) often offer the best rates. Senior citizens (60+) get an extra 0.25% to 0.50% interest on most FDs. Tax-saving FDs have a mandatory 5-year lock-in.

Bank FDs are insured by DICGC (Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation) up to Rs 5 lakh per depositor per bank. This means even if a bank fails, you get back up to Rs 5 lakh. FDs are considered very low risk. NBFC FDs may offer higher rates but carry slightly more risk as DICGC insurance may not apply.

FD interest is fully taxable as per your income tax slab. Banks deduct TDS (Tax Deducted at Source) at 10% if interest exceeds Rs 40,000 per year (Rs 50,000 for senior citizens). If your total income is below taxable limit, submit Form 15G (or 15H for seniors) to avoid TDS. Tax-saving FDs under Section 80C allow deduction up to Rs 1.5 lakh but have 5-year lock-in.

You can break an FD before maturity but banks charge a penalty of 0.5% to 1% on the applicable interest rate. For example, if you booked at 7% and break early, you may get only 6% or 6.5%. Some banks offer no-penalty premature withdrawal for certain FD types. Always check the premature withdrawal clause before booking.

As of 2024, major bank FD rates: SBI offers 6.5%-7.1%, HDFC Bank 7%-7.4%, ICICI Bank 6.7%-7.2%, Axis Bank 6.7%-7.2%, Kotak Mahindra 6.2%-7.4%. Small finance banks like AU Small Finance, Jana Small Finance offer 7.5%-9% but carry slightly higher risk. Senior citizens get 0.25-0.5% extra across all banks.

FD vs Savings Account: FD gives 6-9% vs savings account 2.5-4%. FD vs Mutual Funds: FD is guaranteed return, mutual funds can give higher returns (10-15%) but with market risk. FD vs PPF: PPF gives 7.1% tax-free with 15-year lock-in, FD is taxable but more flexible. FD is best for risk-averse investors who want guaranteed returns.

Steps to book an FD: 1) Choose a bank or NBFC. 2) Decide amount (minimum Rs 1,000 for most banks). 3) Choose tenure. 4) Select interest payout: monthly, quarterly, or at maturity. 5) Provide KYC documents (Aadhaar, PAN). 6) Fund the FD from your savings account. Online FD booking takes less than 5 minutes through net banking or mobile app.

Senior citizens (age 60+) get special FD benefits: Extra 0.25% to 0.50% interest rate over regular rates. Higher TDS exemption threshold of Rs 50,000 per year. Can submit Form 15H to avoid TDS if income is below taxable limit. Some banks offer special senior citizen FD schemes with even higher rates. Always ask specifically for senior citizen FD rates.
